page: Vultr vbm-256c-3072gb-8-mi325x-gpu is a bare metal server configured with dedicated AMD EPYC 9575F processors. Operating on the x86_64 architecture, this system delivers 256 dedicated physical cores and 256 vCPUs at a base frequency of 3.3 GHz. The single-thread-per-core design ensures predictable compute performance without resource sharing or virtualization overhead.

The server is equipped with 3072.0 GB of system memory, providing 12.0 GB of RAM per physical core. For parallel computing workloads, the platform integrates eight AMD MI325X GPUs built on the CDNA3 architecture. These accelerators provide a combined total of 2048 GB of high-bandwidth VRAM, with each individual GPU supplying 256 GB of dedicated video memory.

Local storage consists of 28608 GB of NVMe SSD capacity, designed for high-throughput data access. The server also includes one complimentary public IPv4 address. The massive local storage capacity and high-density memory footprint support large-scale data ingestion and local processing of massive datasets.

This bare metal configuration is designed for large-scale artificial intelligence workloads, deep learning model training, massive LLM inference, and high-performance computing simulations. From a cost-efficiency perspective, the bare metal deployment model eliminates hypervisor overhead, maximizing the return on hardware resources for dense, GPU-heavy, and memory-intensive enterprise workloads.
description: Vultr vbm-256c-3072gb-8-mi325x-gpu is a high-density bare metal server designed for intensive computational workloads. It features 256 dedicated physical cores powered by AMD EPYC 9575F processors running at 3.3 GHz on the x86_64 architecture. The system is equipped with 3072.0 GB of RAM, offering 12.0 GB of memory per core. For acceleration, it integrates eight AMD MI325X GPUs from the CDNA3 family, delivering a total of 2048 GB of VRAM. Storage is supported by 28608 GB of local NVMe SSD capacity. This hardware profile is engineered for large-scale artificial intelligence training, LLM inference, and complex scientific simulations. By deploying on bare metal, the server avoids virtualization overhead, maximizing resource density and computational efficiency for enterprise-grade data science and high-performance computing tasks.
